content.text: Good — `hq-6o7` is mine and in-progress. Let me investigate how `gt rig add` works with external repos.

input.description: Check gt rig add help for external repo support

content.content:
Add a new rig by cloning a repository.

This creates a rig container with:
  - config.json           Rig configuration
  - .beads/               Rig-level issue tracking (initialized)
  - plugins/              Rig-level plugin directory
  - refinery/rig/         Canonical main clone
  - mayor/rig/            Mayor's working clone
  - crew/                 Empty crew directory (add members with 'gt crew add')
  - witness/              Witness agent directory
  - polecats/             Worker directory (empty)

The command also:
  - Seeds patrol molecules (Deacon, Witness, Refinery)
  - Creates ~/gt/plugins/ (town-level) if it doesn't exist
  - Creates <rig>/plugins/ (rig-level)

Use --adopt to register an existing directory instead of creating new:
  - Reads existing config.json if present
  - Auto-detects git URL from origin remote (git-url argument not required)
  - Adds entry to mayor/rigs.json

Example:
  gt rig add gastown https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown
  gt rig add my-project git@github.com:user/repo.git --prefix mp
  gt rig add existing-rig --adopt

Usage:
  gt rig add <name> <git-url> [flags]

Flags:
      --adopt               Adopt an existing directory instead of creating new
      --branch string       Default branch name (default: auto-detected from remote)
      --force               With --adopt, register even if git remote cannot be detected
  -h, --help                help for add
      --local-repo string   Local repo path to share git objects (optional)
      --prefix string       Beads issue prefix (default: derived from name)
      --push-url string     Push URL for read-only upstreams (push to fork)
      --url string          Git remote URL for --adopt (default: auto-detected from origin)
Shell cwd was reset to /home/skogix/gt/mayor

toolUseResult.stdout:
Add a new rig by cloning a repository.

This creates a rig container with:
  - config.json           Rig configuration
  - .beads/               Rig-level issue tracking (initialized)
  - plugins/              Rig-level plugin directory
  - refinery/rig/         Canonical main clone
  - mayor/rig/            Mayor's working clone
  - crew/                 Empty crew directory (add members with 'gt crew add')
  - witness/              Witness agent directory
  - polecats/             Worker directory (empty)

The command also:
  - Seeds patrol molecules (Deacon, Witness, Refinery)
  - Creates ~/gt/plugins/ (town-level) if it doesn't exist
  - Creates <rig>/plugins/ (rig-level)

Use --adopt to register an existing directory instead of creating new:
  - Reads existing config.json if present
  - Auto-detects git URL from origin remote (git-url argument not required)
  - Adds entry to mayor/rigs.json

Example:
  gt rig add gastown https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown
  gt rig add my-project git@github.com:user/repo.git --prefix mp
  gt rig add existing-rig --adopt

Usage:
  gt rig add <name> <git-url> [flags]

Flags:
      --adopt               Adopt an existing directory instead of creating new
      --branch string       Default branch name (default: auto-detected from remote)
      --force               With --adopt, register even if git remote cannot be detected
  -h, --help                help for add
      --local-repo string   Local repo path to share git objects (optional)
      --prefix string       Beads issue prefix (default: derived from name)
      --push-url string     Push URL for read-only upstreams (push to fork)
      --url string          Git remote URL for --adopt (default: auto-detected from origin)

I need to understand how `gt rig add --adopt` works, specifically whether it supports repos outside the ~/gt/ directory (external paths).

4. What happens if the rig directory is outside ~/gt/?

Search broadly - the gastown repo might be at /home/skogix/.local/src/gastown or similar. Also check /home/skogix/gt/ itself for any Go source.

69:Use --adopt to register an existing directory instead of creating new:
77:  gt rig add existing-rig --adopt`,
357:	rigAddCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&rigAddAdopt, "adopt", false, "Adopt an existing directory instead of creating new")
358:	rigAddCmd.Flags().StringVar(&rigAddAdoptURL, "url", "", "Git remote URL for --adopt (default: auto-detected from origin)")
359:	rigAddCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&rigAddAdoptForce, "force", false, "With --adopt, register even if git remote cannot be detected")
470:	// Handle --adopt mode: register existing directory
477:		return fmt.Errorf("git-url is required (or use --adopt to register an existing directory)")
482:		return fmt.Errorf("invalid git URL %q: expected a remote URL (https://, git@, ssh://, git://)\n\nTo register a local directory, use:\n  gt rig add %s --adopt", gitURL, name)
917:		return fmt.Errorf("adopting rig: %w", err)
925:	// Add adopted rig to daemon.json patrol config (witness + refinery rigs arrays)
1009:		// Use mgr.InitBeads() for consistency with the non-adopt path — it handles
1042:	// to match the behavior of the normal (non-adopt) gt rig add path.
1052:	fmt.Printf("\n%s Rig %s adopted\n", style.Success.Render("✓"), name)

content.content:
1→// Package config provides configuration types and serialization for Gas Town.
     2→package config
     3→
     4→import (
     5→	"fmt"
     6→	"os"
     7→	"os/exec"
     8→	"path/filepath"
     9→	"strings"
    10→	"time"
    11→
    12→	"github.com/steveyegge/gastown/internal/scheduler/capacity"
    13→)
    14→
    15→// TownConfig represents the main town identity (mayor/town.json).
    16→type TownConfig struct {
    17→	Type       string    `json:"type"`                  // "town"
    18→	Version    int       `json:"version"`               // schema version
    19→	Name       string    `json:"name"`                  // town identifier (internal)
    20→	Owner      string    `json:"owner,omitempty"`       // owner email (entity identity)
    21→	PublicName string    `json:"public_name,omitempty"` // public display name
    22→	CreatedAt  time.Time `json:"created_at"`
    23→}
    24→
    25→// MayorConfig represents town-level behavioral configuration (mayor/config.json).
    26→// This is separate from TownConfig (identity) to keep configuration concerns distinct.
    27→type MayorConfig struct {
    28→	Type            string           `json:"type"`                        // "mayor-config"
    29→	Version         int              `json:"version"`                     // schema version
    30→	Theme           *TownThemeConfig `json:"theme,omitempty"`             // global theme settings
    31→	Daemon          *DaemonConfig    `json:"daemon,omitempty"`            // daemon settings
    32→	Deacon          *DeaconConfig    `json:"deacon,omitempty"`            // deacon settings
    33→	DefaultCrewName string           `json:"default_crew_name,omitempty"` // default crew name for new rigs
    34→}
    35→
    36→// CurrentTownSettingsVersion is the current schema version for TownSettings.
    37→const CurrentTownSettingsVersion = 1
    38→
    39→// TownSettings represents town-level behavioral configuration (settings/config.json).
    40→// This contains agent configuration that applies to all rigs unless overridden.
    41→type TownSettings struct {
    42→	Type    string `json:"type"`    // "town-settings"
    43→	Version int    `json:"version"` // schema version
    44→
    45→	// CLITheme controls CLI output color scheme.
    46→	// Values: "dark", "light", "auto" (default).
    47→	// "auto" lets the terminal emulator's background color guide the choice.
    48→	// Can be overridden by GT_THEME environment variable.
    49→	CLITheme string `json:"cli_theme,omitempty"`
    50→
    51→	// DefaultAgent is the name of the agent preset to use by default.
    52→	// Can be a built-in preset ("claude", "gemini", "codex", "cursor", "auggie", "amp", "opencode", "copilot")
    53→	// or a custom agent name defined in settings/agents.json.
    54→	// Default: "claude"
    55→	DefaultAgent string `json:"default_agent,omitempty"`
    56→
    57→	// Agents defines custom agent configurations or overrides.
    58→	// Keys are agent names that can be referenced by DefaultAgent or rig settings.
    59→	// Values override or extend the built-in presets.
    60→	// Example: {"gemini": {"command": "/custom/path/to/gemini"}}
    61→	Agents map[string]*RuntimeConfig `json:"agents,omitempty"`
    62→
    63→	// RoleAgents maps role names to agent aliases for per-role model selection.
    64→	// Keys are role names: "mayor", "deacon", "witness", "refinery", "polecat", "crew".
    65→	// Values are agent names (built-in presets or custom agents defined in Agents).
    66→	// This allows cost optimization by using different models for different roles.
    67→	// Example: {"mayor": "claude-opus", "witness": "claude-haiku", "polecat": "claude-sonnet"}
    68→	RoleAgents map[string]string `json:"role_agents,omitempty"`
    69→
    70→	// AgentEmailDomain is the domain used for agent git identity emails.
    71→	// Agent addresses like "gastown/crew/jack" become "gastown.crew.jack@{domain}".
    72→	// Default: "gastown.local"
    73→	AgentEmailDomain string `json:"agent_email_domain,omitempty"`
    74→
    75→	// WebTimeouts configures command execution timeouts for the web dashboard.
    76→	WebTimeouts *WebTimeoutsConfig `json:"web_timeouts,omitempty"`
    77→
    78→	// WorkerStatus configures activity-age thresholds for worker status classification.
    79→	WorkerStatus *WorkerStatusConfig `json:"worker_status,omitempty"`
    80→
    81→	// FeedCurator configures event deduplication and aggregation windows.
    82→	FeedCurator *FeedCuratorConfig `json:"feed_curator,omitempty"`
    83→
    84→	// Convoy configures convoy behavior settings.
    85→	Convoy *ConvoyConfig `json:"convoy,omitempty"`
    86→
    87→	// CostTier tracks which cost tier preset was applied (informational).
    88→	// Actual model assignments live in RoleAgents and Agents.
    89→	// Values: "standard", "economy", "budget", or empty for custom configs.
    90→	CostTier string `json:"cost_tier,omitempty"`
    91→
    92→	// Scheduler configures the capacity scheduler for polecat dispatch.
    93→	Scheduler *capacity.SchedulerConfig `json:"scheduler,omitempty"`
    94→}
    95→
    96→// NewTownSettings creates a new TownSettings with defaults.
    97→func NewTownSettings() *TownSettings {
    98→	return &TownSettings{
    99→		Type:         "town-settings",
   100→		Version:      CurrentTownSettingsVersion,
   101→		DefaultAgent: "claude",
   102→		Agents:       make(map[string]*RuntimeConfig),
   103→		RoleAgents:   make(map[string]string),
   104→	}
   105→}
   106→
   107→// WebTimeoutsConfig configures command execution timeouts for the web dashboard.
   108→type WebTimeoutsConfig struct {
   109→	// CmdTimeout is the timeout for bd (beads) commands. Default: "15s".
   110→	CmdTimeout string `json:"cmd_timeout,omitempty"`
   111→	// GhCmdTimeout is the timeout for GitHub API commands. Default: "10s".
   112→	GhCmdTimeout string `json:"gh_cmd_timeout,omitempty"`
   113→	// TmuxCmdTimeout is the timeout for tmux queries. Default: "2s".
   114→	TmuxCmdTimeout string `json:"tmux_cmd_timeout,omitempty"`
   115→	// FetchTimeout is the maximum time for all dashboard data fetches. Default: "8s".
   116→	FetchTimeout string `json:"fetch_timeout,omitempty"`
   117→	// DefaultRunTimeout is the default timeout for /api/run commands. Default: "30s".
   118→	DefaultRunTimeout string `json:"default_run_timeout,omitempty"`
   119→	// MaxRunTimeout is the maximum allowed timeout for /api/run commands. Default: "60s".
   120→	MaxRunTimeout string `json:"max_run_timeout,omitempty"`
   121→}
   122→
   123→// DefaultWebTimeoutsConfig returns a WebTimeoutsConfig with sensible defaults.
   124→func DefaultWebTimeoutsConfig() *WebTimeoutsConfig {
   125→	return &WebTimeoutsConfig{
   126→		CmdTimeout:        "15s",
   127→		GhCmdTimeout:      "10s",
   128→		TmuxCmdTimeout:    "2s",
   129→		FetchTimeout:      "8s",
   130→		DefaultRunTimeout: "30s",
   131→		MaxRunTimeout:     "60s",
   132→	}
   133→}
   134→
   135→// WorkerStatusConfig configures activity-age thresholds for worker status classification.
   136→type WorkerStatusConfig struct {
   137→	// StaleThreshold is the activity age after which a worker is considered "stale".
   138→	// Default: "5m".
   139→	StaleThreshold string `json:"stale_threshold,omitempty"`
   140→	// StuckThreshold is the activity age after which a worker is considered "stuck".
   141→	// Default: "30m".
   142→	StuckThreshold string `json:"stuck_threshold,omitempty"`
   143→	// HeartbeatFreshThreshold is the max age for a Deacon heartbeat to be considered fresh.
   144→	// Default: "5m".
   145→	HeartbeatFreshThreshold string `json:"heartbeat_fresh_threshold,omitempty"`
   146→	// MayorActiveThreshold is the max session inactivity for the Mayor to be considered active.
   147→	// Default: "5m".
   148→	MayorActiveThreshold string `json:"mayor_active_threshold,omitempty"`
   149→}
   150→

data.prompt:
I need to understand how `gt rig add --adopt` works, specifically whether it supports repos outside the ~/gt/ directory (external paths). 

Search the gastown source code. The `gt` binary is likely built from a Go codebase. Check:
1. Find the source for `gt rig add` command - look in /home/skogix/.local/src/ or anywhere Go source might live
2. How does --adopt work? Does it create symlinks? Copy? Just register?
3. Is there any "port" or external path concept already implemented?
4. What happens if the rig directory is outside ~/gt/?

Search broadly - the gastown repo might be at /home/skogix/.local/src/gastown or similar. Also check /home/skogix/gt/ itself for any Go source.

278:type RigsConfig struct {
279-	Version int                 `json:"version"`
280-	Rigs    map[string]RigEntry `json:"rigs"`
281-}
282-
283-// RigEntry represents a single rig in the registry.
284:type RigEntry struct {
285-	GitURL      string       `json:"git_url"`
286-	PushURL     string       `json:"push_url,omitempty"`
287-	LocalRepo   string       `json:"local_repo,omitempty"`
288-	AddedAt     time.Time    `json:"added_at"`
289-	BeadsConfig *BeadsConfig `json:"beads,omitempty"`
290-}
291-
292-// BeadsConfig represents beads configuration for a rig.
293-type BeadsConfig struct {
294-	Repo   string `json:"repo"`   // "local" | path | git-url
295-	Prefix string `json:"prefix"` // issue prefix
296-}
297-
298-// CurrentTownVersion is the current schema version for TownConfig.
299-// Version 2: Added Owner and PublicName fields for federation identity.
300-const CurrentTownVersion = 2
301-
302-// CurrentRigsVersion is the current schema version for RigsConfig.
303-const CurrentRigsVersion = 1
304-
